# Gamification and Game-Based Learning

Games can introduce goals, interaction, feedback, problem solving, competition, narrative, and fun learning environments, elements that can increase learner engagement and sustain motivation. This teaching tip discusses the difference between gamification and game-based learning, the pedagogical values these two strategies can bring to instruction, and game elements appropriate for face-to-face and online courses.

## Gamification vs. game-based learning

Gamification and game-based learning are similar in that both strategies promote engagement and sustained motivation in learning. However, gamification and game-based learning can also be usefully distinguished:

**Gamification** is the integration of game elements like point systems, leaderboards, badges, or other elements related to games into “conventional” learning activities in order to increase engagement and motivation. For example, an online discussion forum for a Physics course might be gamified via a badge system: students might be awarded a “Ptolemy” badge after they have made 10 postings, a “Galileo” badge after 20 postings, “Kepler” after 30, “Einstein” after 40, and so on. In ideal gamified learning environments, students can see the online badges that their peers have earned to create a sense of comradery or competition.

**Game-based learning**, in contrast, involves designing learning activities so that game characteristics and game principles inhere within the learning activities themselves. For example, in an Economics course, students might compete in a virtual stock-trading competition; in a Political Science course, students might role-play as they engage in mock negotiations involving a labour dispute.

In short, gamification applies game elements or a game framework to existing learning activities; game-based learning designs learning activities that are intrinsically game-like.

Gamification and game-based learning both promote engagement and sustained motivation in learning, but they do not necessarily result in improved learning outcomes. For a detailed explanation between these two approaches, and for helpful videos and other resources, consider looking at [GOBLIN (Games Offer Bold Learning Insights Nowadays) Education.](http://goblin.education/gamification/)

## Game elements and their pedagogical role

Most games feature elements such as rules, goals, interaction, feedback, problem solving, competition, story, and fun (see Vandercruysse, Vandewaetere, & Clarebout, 2012). Though not all of the elements are needed to successfully gamify a learning activity, carefully selecting those elements that help meet the learning objectives of the course can be useful. The pedagogical value of game features often associated with gamification are discussed below.

### Points or experience systems

Similar to conventional grading schemes, game points or experience (XP) systems reward students for completing various tasks, assignments, or assessments. Game or XP points can introduce some useful affordances to learning environments, including:

Points can also be supplemented by academic rewards: when a certain point threshold is reached, a student might be given an extra week to submit an assignment or bonus questions on the next test.

### Badges

Badges are a digital way to acknowledge student work. For example, students might receive a badge if they achieve certain levels of success on assignments, or if they do additional work, such as submitting a draft or sharing notes with another student. They may even be the result of simple participation: accessing the course through the LMS five times a week over the course of a semester could earn a badge. Student badges may be displayed to other learners in the class as a means to encourage competition or to demonstrate the variety of badges which can be earned.

Create your own badges for your course with free programs such as [Credly](https://credly.com/) or [OpenBadges](https://www.openbadges.me/).

### Leaderboards

Competition can motivate students and can be leveraged by leaderboards that showcase the distribution of point totals that students have accumulated through various learning activities. However, caution must be taken when constructing leaderboards because displaying all students in order of point totals can be a disincentive for students at the bottom (Farzen, DiMicco, Millen, Dugan, Geyer & Brownholtz, 2008; Landers & Landers, 2015). Consider using a system in which students see only the two students who are directly above them and below them, in order to foster a healthy sense of competition without discouraging students who are performing poorly.

For examples as to how to create a leaderboard, see [How to Create a Leaderboard for eLearning with Google](https://blog.elblearning.com/blog/how-to-create-a-leaderboard-elearning-google).

## Online gamification strategies

### Discussion boards

Students may be incentivized to discuss readings before or immediately after class by making them optional but allotting XP points for each post or reply to another post. Points earned could go towards additional help on an assignment (such as allowing a draft to be reviewed first, or having an automatic extension granted), or to completely bypassing an assignment (if the student reaches a certain number of points, they no longer have to complete a specific assignment in the course). You can also facilitate real-time discussions using tools like [Padlet](https://padlet.com/), which allow students to make anonymous contributions.

### Quizzes

Rather than presenting a set of seemingly unrelated questions, consider creating a narrative or quest that draws learners in and helps them see the consequence of their responses. Think of your quiz as an interactive narrative: each question leads into the next, and may build upon previous answers, all the while being part of a larger narrative or story that compels the learner to remain engaged. You may even include hints that the learner can choose to use or not. Be cautious of making these high-stakes assessments – the game-like features of the task will be mitigated and potentially impaired should learners be focused too much on the grade attached. An alternative method is to have students complete a quiz in the online environment, and then come to class, form groups, and complete the same quiz, but now try to convince one another of the correct answer. This can be facilitated through [Immediate Feedback Assessment Technique](http://testmaker.if-at.com/home/Default.aspx) (IF-AT) cards too.

## In-class gamification strategies

### Jeopardy

Creating Jeopardy-style games for review of chapters or in preparation for a midterm can provide enjoyment and interaction with others in a familiar game structure. Create a game using PowerPoint, or use a free Jeopardy game creator like [Instant Jeopardy Review](http://www.superteachertools.us/jeopardyx/).

### Classroom response systems

Gathering responses from an entire class can be difficult, but doing so with classroom response systems like [Kahoot](https://getkahoot.com/) or [TopHat](https://app.tophat.com/) can encourage participation through game elements like points and competition between individuals.

## Out-of-class gamification strategies

### Game-based learning environments

These include any game designed for educational purposes such as [Trivia Crack](http://www.triviacrack.com/). As an instructor, you can make learners aware of these educational games, but the game itself acts as the educator.

### Game-enhanced learning environments

These environments employ commercially-available games that are designed with entertainment in mind. Learners play these games for fun, and must then be provided or find means by which to discuss gameplay experiences with like-minded individuals. As an instructor, your role can be to provide or invite examples of games that are related to the discipline, and importantly, provide the space for learners to reflect on their gameplay. Massively Multiplayer Online Role-playing Games like *World of Warcraft*or *Second Life*are great for immersing language learners in another language, or games like *SimCity*can help understand economic principles.

## Some final notes

Incorporating games, gamification, or game-based learning into your teaching doesn’t require a monumental shift in your teaching. Many activities or [active learning strategies](/centre-for-teaching-excellence/catalogs/tip-sheets/active-learning-activities) that you already use likely contain some of the game elements listed above and, with some modifications, can be modified into even more effective learning tools. At the same time, gamification and game-based learning should not be implemented in a cavalier manner, but should be thoughtfully integrated into a course.

# Enhancing Learner Engagement And Motivation With eLearning Gamification

![Learner Engagement And Motivation With Gamification](https://cdn.elearningindustry.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Learner-Engagement-And-Motivation-With-Gamification.jpg)
![Photo of Sammy Stahl](https://cdn.elearningindustry.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/93371fc3f91d6edd17df9d96b46379e4.gif)
![Level 1]()
![Level 2]()

## Using Gamification For Learner Engagement And Motivation

In the context of eLearning, gamification involves incorporating elements such as points, badges, leaderboards, levels, challenges, and rewards into educational content. Learners are motivated by competition, immediate feedback, personalized learning paths, and a sense of community. These elements create a feeling of competition, achievement, and progress, making the learning experience more immersive and enjoyable.

### How Gamification Can Enhance Learner Engagement And Motivation

In recent years, gamification has emerged as a powerful tool in the field of eLearning, revolutionizing the way learners engage with course material. By incorporating game elements and mechanics into educational content, gamification has proven to enhance learner engagement and motivation. This article explores the concept of gamification in eLearning and its impact on learners, highlighting its benefits and providing insights into its implementation.

To put it briefly, gamification in learning has the following advantages:

### Exploring The Benefits Of Gamified Learning In Detail

#### 1. Learner Engagement

Enhancing learner engagement is one of the main advantages of gamification in eLearning. Traditional teaching techniques frequently need help grabbing and holding students' attention. However, eLearning platforms can improve the interaction and enjoyment of the learning experience by incorporating game components. When students actively participate in the learning process and can see their progress through the use of points, badges, and levels, they are more likely to remain motivated and interested.

#### 2. Learner Motivation

Additionally, by encouraging competitiveness among students, gamification increases their drive to succeed. Leaderboards and challenges motivate students to evaluate their performance in comparison to their peers, fostering a positive environment of competition. This not only drives learners to put in more effort but also promotes collaboration and social interaction as they strive to outperform others. As a result, students are motivated to work harder and are also encouraged to collaborate and communicate with others.

#### 3. Immediate Feedback

Moreover, gamification in eLearning provides learners with immediate feedback, which is crucial for their growth and development. In traditional learning methods, learners often have to wait for assessments or assignments to receive feedback on their progress. However, with gamification, learners receive instant feedback on their performance, allowing them to identify their strengths and areas for improvement. This real-time feedback enables learners to make adjustments and enhance their learning experience, leading to better learning outcomes.

#### 4. Personalized Learning

The capacity of gamification in eLearning to encourage personalized learning is a key additional benefit. Gamification allows students to choose the pace and course of their learning. They can pick their educational pathways, investigate various subjects, and receive awards based on their accomplishments. This feeling of independence empowers students and makes learning more fun. By altering the level of difficulty based on learners' performance, gamification also enables adaptive learning, making sure that they are adequately challenged and motivated. This personalized approach also promotes self-reflection, enables learners to take ownership of their progress, and increases engagement by addressing their individual needs.

### Aspects To Consider When Implementing Gamification In eLearning

Gamification in [eLearning implementation](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/265877898_Raising_engagement_in_e-learning_through_gamification "eLearning") needs careful planning and design. The game's components must align with the lesson's goals and content. Instead of detracting from or obscuring the educational content, the gaming mechanisms should enhance it. Additionally, it is essential to balance competitiveness with cooperation to foster a healthy learning environment.

Many eLearning platforms have successfully integrated gamification into their courses, demonstrating its effectiveness. For example, Duolingo, a popular language learning platform, utilizes gamification elements such as points, leaderboards, and streaks to motivate learners to practice and improve their language skills consistently. By making the learning process feel like a game, Duolingo has attracted millions of users worldwide. This is something that educational institutions can try to incorporate into their curriculums.

### Final Thoughts

Gamification in eLearning has become a potent technique for raising motivation and engagement among students. eLearning platforms can enhance the learning process by adding gaming mechanics and components, making it more interactive and gratifying. As such, gamification promotes individualized learning, encourages competitiveness, offers immediate feedback, and generates a sense of community among students. Gamification has the potential to completely transform the way we learn by increasing the accessibility and effectiveness of instruction.

**Revitalizing Educational Experiences with Gamification for Learning**
-----------------------------------------------------------------------

Gamification for learning is one of the most powerful ways for students, employees, and others to achieve educational goals. It has the power to completely transform ordinary learning environments and turn them into motivating, engaging, and immersive experiences for learners.

When used correctly, gamification for learning has been proven to [improve test scores](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9589250/#:~:text=Through%20practice%2C%20it%20has%20been,improving%20the%20students'%20academic%20performance.) and general [academic performance](https://mdpi-res.com/d_attachment/sustainability/sustainability-14-05115/article_deploy/sustainability-14-05115.pdf?version=1650786075). It has also been shown to enhance [motivation and group work](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC9880393/).

We’ll take a deeper look at gamification in learning, including its multiple benefits, how to implement gamification for learning effectively, practical examples, and more.

**What Is Gamification for Learning?**
--------------------------------------

Gamification for learning is based on applying techniques from the world of gaming to educational goals, which tap into intrinsic and extrinsic motivators. These can include elements such as leaderboards, rewards in the form of points or coins, advancing through levels, earning badges, and friendly competition with peers.

**Benefits of Gamification for Learning**
-----------------------------------------

It’s easy to see the immediate benefits of gamification for learning; learners are more motivated and excited about the material. There are also many other, less obvious benefits.

**Improved learner engagement** The interactive and immersive nature of advanced gamification makes the learning process more enjoyable and encourages long-term active participation.

**Increased motivation** Elements such as[points, badges, levels, and rewards](https://centrical.com/platform/gamification/) create a sense of achievement and progress for learners. They also increase motivation to actively pursue goals, complete tasks, and overcome challenges, often leading to improved learning (and performance) outcomes.

**Enhanced retention and knowledge application** By applying knowledge and skills in practical scenarios, and periodically reinforcing that knowledge through quizzes and knowledge checks, learners are more likely to retain information and understand how to use it in real-world contexts.

**Immediate feedback and progress tracking** Gamification platforms can provide learners with immediate feedback on their performance, helping them stay motivated, understand gaps, and adjust their learning strategies where necessary.

**Social interaction and collaboration** Leading gamified learning platforms incorporate advanced social features such as segmented leaderboards, multiplayer modes, or team-based challenges, as well as [social sharing capabilities](https://centrical.com/resources/how-employee-contributions-boost-to-team-performance/). This fosters collaboration, competition, and peer interaction, allowing learners to learn from – and with – others.

**Personalized learning experiences** Gamification can be adapted to accommodate different learning styles, preferences, and skill levels. Learners can progress at their own pace, receive personalized challenges, and [access tailored content](https://centrical.com/platform/microlearning/), creating a more individualized learning experience.

**Real-time assessment**When using a platform with robust analytics and data tracking, educators can gather insights into learners’ progress, identify areas of struggle, and adjust instructional strategies accordingly.

Overall, gamification for learning can make the learning process more enjoyable, engaging, and effective, leading to improved knowledge retention, skill development, and motivation among learners.

![Image 60](https://centrical.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/AdobeStock_407384172-1024x683.jpeg)

**How to Use Gamification for Learning: 7 Top Strategies**
----------------------------------------------------------

While it’s easy to get excited about unlocking the benefits that gamification for learning offers, actually implementing effective strategies to access these benefits. Here are 7 top strategies to effectively incorporate gamification for learning:

1.   **Set clear objectives** Start by defining clear learning objectives that align with the desired outcomes. Identify the specific knowledge, skills, or behaviors you want learners to acquire through the gamified learning experience. This will guide the design and implementation of the gamification elements.

1.   **Identify relevant game elements** Select game elements that are appropriate for the learning context and objectives. This may include points, badges, leaderboards, levels, challenges, virtual rewards, and storytelling. Consider how each element can enhance learner engagement and motivation while reinforcing the desired learning outcomes

1.   **Provide meaningful feedback** Incorporate immediate and constructive feedback throughout the gamified learning experience. Feedback should be clear, relevant, and timely. It can be in the form of visual cues, progress bars, scores, coaching, or narrative feedback. This helps learners track their progress, make adjustments, and reinforce their learning.

1.   **Offer a sense of progression** Design the gamified learning experience with an aim to provide a sense of achievement and progression. Divide the content or tasks into levels, stages, or modules that gradually increase in difficulty or complexity. As learners progress, they should feel a sense of accomplishment and see their skills and knowledge develop.

1.   **Foster****competition and collaboration** Utilize both competitive (within reason) and collaborative elements to promote engagement and social interaction. Leaderboards can create healthy competition among learners, while collaborative activities or team challenges encourage cooperation and peer learning. 

1.   **Personalize the learning experience** Provide opportunities for learners to personalize their experience. Allow them to select learning paths based on their interests or skill levels, as well as choose avatars and customize their profiles. Personalization enhances engagement and ownership, as learners feel a sense of autonomy and control over their learning journey.

1.   **Track and analyze learner data** Leverage data tracking and analytics to monitor learner progress and identify areas for improvement. Collect data on performance, engagement levels, completion rates, and challenges. Analyzing this data can help identify patterns, adjust instructional strategies, and provide targeted support to learners.

Remember that gamification [should go beyond the leaderboard](https://centrical.com/resources/gamification-for-employee-engagement/) and be integrated thoughtfully and purposefully into the learning experience. It should align with the learning objectives, provide meaningful engagement, and support the desired learning outcomes. Regularly evaluate and iterate on the gamification strategy based on learner feedback and data analysis to continuously improve the effectiveness of the approach.

![Image 61](https://centrical.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/AdobeStock_236296074-1024x684.jpeg)

**Implementing Gamification for Learning Environments**
-------------------------------------------------------

Implementing gamification in learning environments requires careful planning and execution to ensure its effectiveness. It’s crucial to clearly define the learning objectives and identify the specific game elements that align with those objectives.

Once the game elements and structure are established, it’s important to effectively integrate gamification into the learning environment. This includes providing adequate training and support to both educators and learners to ensure a smooth transition. Educators/learning and development teams should be equipped with the necessary tools and resources to manage and facilitate the gamified learning experience. Clear instructions and guidelines should be communicated to learners, outlining the purpose and expectations of the gamification approach.

**Evaluating the Impact of Gamification for Learning**
------------------------------------------------------

Evaluating the impact of gamification for learning involves defining evaluation criteria and collecting both quantitative and qualitative data. Criteria should be established to measure aspects such as learner engagement, motivation, knowledge acquisition and retention, skill development, learning outcomes, and any other company-specific KPIs.

Data collection can include metrics like completion rates, scores, and time spent on tasks, as well as surveys, interviews, or focus groups to gather qualitative insights. Testing with a comparison with non-gamified approaches helps establish a baseline and identify the unique benefits of gamification.

Analyzing learner performance data and seeking feedback from learners provides valuable information on the impact of gamification. Based on the evaluation results, adjustments can be made to game elements and instructional strategies to optimize the impact of gamification for learning.

Continuous evaluation and refinement are crucial. Regular assessment of the impact of gamification, along with adjustments based on the evaluation findings, ensures ongoing improvement of the gamified learning experience. By systematically evaluating the impact of gamification, educators can gain insights into its effectiveness, make data-driven decisions, and create a more engaging and effective learning environment.

![Image 62](https://centrical.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/04/AdobeStock_370808984-1024x683.jpeg)

**Examples of Gamification for Learning**
-----------------------------------------

[Gamification for learning](https://centrical.com/resources/gamification-examples/) can take many different forms and be used in almost any context.

For example, gamification for learning is used in the classroom to achieve desired educational outcomes in a fun, engaging way. Educators can assign tasks, set up friendly competitions, and turn traditional learning into something fun, memorable, and effective.

SUMMARY:
The main arguments for the implementation of game elements or even full-fledged games as an educational tool are based on potentially fostering learning outcomes (Plass et al., 2020; Wouters & van Oostendorp, 2013) via their capability to increase engagement and motivation, and to enhance learner (or user) experience (Zainuddin et al., 2020). Hence, a careful balance between game elements and educational content is required to keep learning tasks attractive, effective, and engaging at the same time (Plass et al., 2020). Interest and motivation are closely related to engagement (Axelson & Flick, 2010; Christenson et al., 2012), which has been defined as “the active and focused investment of effort in a game environment” (Schwartz & Plass, 2020), and is of main interest in the current study which investigates the effects of game elements on learners in an online learning environment.

Highlights
----------

*   •Relations between game elements, engagement, and learning outcomes are examined 
*   •Use of game elements mitigates participant dropout early in an online learning task 
*   •Learning outcomes are affected by game elements indirectly via task engagement 
*   •Participants with lower self-efficacy may benefit more from game elements 
*   •Performance gain mediated by task attractivity balances cognitive cost 

Abstract
--------

A growing body of literature suggests that adding game elements to learning tasks indirectly influences the learning process by increasing engagement with the tasks. The present study aims to advance learning engagement research by examining an often neglected subcomponent of behavioral engagement, attrition. Implementing two equivalent versions of a learning task, differing solely in the presence of game elements, allowed unequivocal attribution of any effect on the presence of game elements. Conducting the study in an online learning environment allowed further a highly unconstrained examination of the effects of game elements on attrition. We found that game elements affected both participant attrition and engagement of participants who completed the learning task. Participants with low self-efficacy were particularly prone to drop out in the non-game condition. Game elements also affected both learning efficacy and efficiency. We further found task attractivity to partially mediate the effect of game elements on learning outcomes. The results suggest that by facilitating engagement via task attractivity game elements can compensate to some extent for the increased cognitive demand that the game elements induce. We finally discuss the importance of considering the interrelations between learner characteristics, game elements, and engagement for interpreting results on [learning performance](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/learning-performance "Learn more about learning performance from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") measures.

1. Introduction
---------------

The interest in how learning can be affected by the inclusion of game elements in [digital learning](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/digital-education "Learn more about digital learning from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") tasks has grown over the last decades ([Wouters & van Oostendorp, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib100)). The main arguments for the implementation of game elements or even full-fledged games as an educational tool are based on potentially fostering learning outcomes ([Plass et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib75); [Wouters & van Oostendorp, 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib99)) via their capability to increase engagement and motivation, and to enhance learner (or user) experience ([Zainuddin et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib102)). To this aim, game elements such as visual and auditory aesthetics, rewards, narrative or game fantasy are frequently employed in the design of learning tasks ([Bedwell et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib17)). Albeit their use in making learning situations more appealing, game elements might also hinder learning, by overloading limited cognitive capacities ([Baddeley, 1992](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib15); [Chandler & Sweller, 1991](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib26); [Mayer, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib69)), resulting in the [seductive detail](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/seductive-detail "Learn more about seductive detail from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") effect ([Bender et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib20); [Rey, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib82)). Hence, a careful balance between game elements and educational content is required to keep learning tasks attractive, effective, and engaging at the same time ([Plass et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib75)).

### 1.1. Cognition, affect, motivation, and engagement in learning with multimedia

Research focusing on cognitive aspects of learning with multimedia, like [cognitive load theory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/cognitive-load-theory "Learn more about cognitive load theory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CLT; [Sweller, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib92)) or the [cognitive theory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/cognitive-theory "Learn more about cognitive theory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of multimedia learning ([Mayer, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib69)), emphasize keeping learning material plain to avoid extraneous [cognitive processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/cognitive-process "Learn more about cognitive processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). In line with this view, additional design elements, not inherently required for task accomplishment, were shown to be capable of impeding learning by acting as seductive details ([Bender et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib20); [Rey, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib82)). The latter attract and hold the learners’ attention, thus distracting them from the task and its efficient accomplishment.

However, not all additional task elements lead to the seductive detail effect. For instance, emotional design elements were shown to facilitate learning ([Wong & Adesope, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib97)). Further, design elements affecting motivation can also indirectly affect learning outcomes ([Huang & Mayer, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib46)). For instance, the inclusion of game elements in learning tasks is typically justified from an emotional design perspective by referring to their potential to increase the learners’ engagement with the task ([Ge & Ifenthaler, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib38); [Plass et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib75)). Disengagement from a task, on the other hand, has been reported to be less likely if game elements are present ([Bernecker & Ninaus, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib106)).

Hence, cognitive, affective, and motivational aspects seem highly interrelated in multimedia learning. The Integrated Cognitive Affective Model of Learning with Multimedia (ICALM; [Plass & Kaplan, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib77)) provides a theoretical framework for capturing this intricate interplay between design elements, cognitive, affective and motivational factors, and learning outcomes in digital learning environments. Regarding cognition, the ICALM is similar to its conceptual predecessors (CLT, see e.g., [Sweller, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib92); the [cognitive theory](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/cognitive-theory "Learn more about cognitive theory from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of multimedia learning, see e.g., [Mayer, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib69)). The two central components for [cognitive processing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/cognitive-process "Learn more about cognitive processing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") along verbal and non-verbal channels are working memory and long-term memory. Working memory is highly limited regarding the amount of information and the duration for which information can be stored. However, [processing information](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/information-processing "Learn more about processing information from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") via working memory is necessary for transferring it to long-term memory with presumably unlimited capacity. Long-term memory can, in reverse, affect working memory by allowing efficient processing using, for instance, chunking mechanisms, providing a conceptual framework for the effects of [prior knowledge](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/prior-knowledge "Learn more about prior knowledge from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

The ICALM, however, provides an additional channel influencing memory: [affective states](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/emotion "Learn more about affective states from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Plass & Kalyuga, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib76)). A crucial point in the ICALM is that cognitive processes are regarded as emerging from the interplay of prior knowledge, abilities, beliefs, affect, and motivation ([Cloude et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib29)). That also implies that cognitive processes are inseparably intertwined with affective processes, experienced as [moods](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/mood "Learn more about moods from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or emotions, when learners process multimedia content ([Plass & Kaplan, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib77)). Consequently, the model emphasizes that also affective processes demand cognitive resources, which should be considered in instructional design. According to the ICALM, affect that involves appraisal is subjectively experienced as interest or motivation influencing the construction of mental representations as well as influencing what elements of the multimedia material are processed. Interest and motivation are closely related to engagement ([Axelson & Flick, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib14); [Christenson et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib28)), which has been defined as “the active and focused investment of effort in a game environment” ([Schwartz & Plass, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib87)), and is of main interest in the current study which investigates the effects of game elements on learners in an online learning environment.

### 1.2. Engagement and motivation in game-based learning

In a [systematic review](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/systematic-review "Learn more about systematic review from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") specifically focusing on the effectiveness of game features in digital tasks for enhancing engagement with online programs, [Looyestyn et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib66) reported a medium to large effect on behavioral engagement metrics (e.g., time spent with a learning resource). More recently, [Sailer and Homner (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib85) reported a small effect of the use of game design elements on motivational learning outcomes (e.g., engagement, self-efficacy, preferences, intrinsic motivation) but noted a high degree of heterogeneity in effect sizes. Overall, the authors of both studies noted several limitations of the generalizability of their conclusions due to the broad diversity of characteristics of the analyzed studies, like the type of the game, the domain, the experimental design, or the employed instructional technique. In addition, tracing back effects on learning to specific game elements is often difficult, because of the multitude of factors that could, in principle, affect the learning process and its outcomes ([Boyle et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib104); [Sailer & Homner, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib85)). [Sailer and Homner (2020)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib85), for instance, noted that participants’ characteristics such as differences in the individual perception of game design elements, [personality traits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/social-sciences/personality-trait "Learn more about personality traits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), or player types could not be included in their analysis, because they are not examined in the included studies of their meta-analysis in the first place, but could account for some of the variance in the obtained effect sizes.

Regarding learning and educational environments, [Fredricks et al. (2004)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib36) discuss the importance of the concept of engagement in schools due to the high rates of boredom, disaffection, and dropout of students and its consequences for learning. The adoption of games as an instructional approach has repeatedly been justified by their potential to increase learners’ engagement and, consequently, to enhance learning outcomes ([Cheng & Su, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib27); [Ge & Ifenthaler, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib38); [Plass et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib75)). Measuring engagement is complicated by the fact that it is intricately related to many concepts like motivation, emotions, affect, attention, and concentration. To resolve the resulting ambiguity and overlap of concepts, a multidimensional perspective of engagement decomposed into behavioral, cognitive, and affective engagement is widely accepted ([Fredricks et al., 2004](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib36); [Ge & Ifenthaler, 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib38); [Plass et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib74), [2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib75)).

Behavioral engagement encompasses the physical actions of learners such as gestures, movements while using a mouse, pressing the keyboard, or even full-body movements in motion-sensing input devices executed during an interaction with a gamified learning task ([Plass et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib74)). Cognitive engagement encompasses the deliberate and active investment of mental efforts that allows learners to select (address processing of relevant material), organize (arrange the content into a coherent mental structure), and integrate (associate the incoming content to prior knowledge) learning material. Cognitive engagement is closely related to the concept of need for cognition ([Ke et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib55)), which refers to an individual's general inclination towards and pleasure in challenging cognitive activities ([Cacioppo et al., 1996](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib25)). Affective engagement is described as learners' emotional responses to game elements aiming to improve their situational experience and maintain the willingness to invest high cognitive efforts over extended periods. Accordingly, engagement is thus often thought of in terms of action or, more specifically, the behavioral, cognitive, and affective manifestation of motivation ([Fredricks & McColskey, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib37); [Skinner et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib88)). In the framework of the ICALM ([Plass & Kaplan, 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib77)), engagement can hence be considered as the behavioral, cognitive, and affective components that mediate effects of interest and motivation on verbal and visual information processing during learning in digital environments.

Studies have demonstrated that different game elements are effective in promoting different types of engagement and enhancing learning ([Abdul Jabbar & Felicia, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib11)). For instance, game elements such as scaffolding, and control or choices are known as game features able to enhance behavioral engagement ([Abdul Jabbar & Felicia, 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib11); [Lavoué et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib61); [Schwartz & Plass, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib87)). Narrative, visual and auditory aesthetics, virtual environments, and avatars are often discussed as features enhancing emotional engagement ([Schwartz & Plass, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib87); [Lavoué et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib61); [Ninaus et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib105)). Incentive systems like rewards or scores are known to be capable of enhancing both behavioral and emotional engagement ([Schwartz & Plass, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib87)).

Any form of engagement with a learning task is an essential prerequisite for learning to happen. Engagement with a task, however, requires participants not only to maintain their attention on the task, but also to regulate aversive feelings (like boredom) associated with it, and generally resist the temptation of engaging in something more pleasurable instead ([Kurzban et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib58); [Miller et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib73)). In short: Engagement with a task can be typically conceptualized as conflicting with opportunities associated with alternative activities. The _Opportunity Cost Model of Subjective Effort and Task Performance_ ([Kurzban et al., 2013](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib58)) has related this motivational conflict to both performance and subjective experience of effort. Interestingly, the integration of game elements or attributes into conventional (cognitive) tasks, has been shown to reduce [task disengagement](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/psychology/task-disengagement "Learn more about task disengagement from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Bernecker & Ninaus, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib106)). Game elements – among other things – seem to improve user experience (e.g., attractiveness of the task; [Javora et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib51); [Ninaus et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib109)), which can increase participants’ (intrinsic) motivation to stay on the task at hand ([Javora et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib51)).

### 1.3. Attrition as an important subcomponent of engagement

The discontinuation of a task is often referred to as attrition. More specifically, attrition is the gradual loss of participants over the course of a study or intervention and can be considered as a subcomponent of task (dis-)engagement ([Lumsden et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib67)). Therefore, measuring attrition rate (e.g., when do participants cease to interact with the task) serves as an objective measure of this component of behavioral engagement, which can be a result of lacking intrinsic motivation (e.g., [Suárez et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib91)). In the laboratory, attrition rates are typically low due to demand characteristics, politeness expectations, obedience to authority, and conformity norms ([Hoerger, 2010](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib44); [Skitka & Sargis, 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib89)). In contrast, dropout rates of the order of 50% have been reported in online intervention studies ([Kelders et al., 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib54); [Wangberg et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib94)) and especially early dropout proves challenging for online studies ([Eysenbach, 2005](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0747563223002996#bib33)). For its relations to engagement, accounting for attrition systematically seems especially important for the investigation of effects of game elements on learning. SUMMARY:
Instead of simply completing assignments or studying for tests, students are rewarded for their efforts with things like virtual badges, progress bars, or even a chance to “level up.” These rewards provide additional, attainable goals that motivate students and make learning feel more like a fun challenge than a chore. *   **Increases student engagement**: Gamification turns learning into an interactive and fun experience by using elements like points, badges, and challenges. Now that you understand how gamification can transform learning, here are some practical and effective strategies that you can use in the classroom on how to gamify your classroom to make lessons more engaging and fun for your students. At the same time, you can create team challenges where students work together to solve problems or complete tasks, earning shared rewards like group badges or class privileges.

10 Effective Examples of Gamification in the Classroom
======================================================

![Image 1](https://pce.sandiego.edu/wp-content/uploads/2024/12/gamification-in-education-3.jpg)

Blog Post 
###### 10 Effective Examples of Gamification in the Classroom

Overview

*   [What Is Gamification in Education?](https://pce.sandiego.edu/gamification-in-education/#what-is)
*   [Gamification vs. Game-Based Learning](https://pce.sandiego.edu/gamification-in-education/#gamification-vs-game-based-learning)
*   [Key Benefits of Gamifying Educational Experiences](https://pce.sandiego.edu/gamification-in-education/#key-benefits-of-gamifying-educational-experiences)
*   [10 Examples of Effective Gamification Strategies in Classrooms](https://pce.sandiego.edu/gamification-in-education/#10-examples-of-effective-gamification-strategies-in-classrooms)
*   [Challenges and Considerations When Using Gamification](https://pce.sandiego.edu/gamification-in-education/#challenges-and-considerations-when-using-gamification)
*   [Frequently Asked Questions](https://pce.sandiego.edu/gamification-in-education/#frequently-asked-questions)

Think back to when you were a kid. Remember how exciting it felt to get a gold star on your homework or a sticker for good behavior? Those little rewards made you feel proud, motivated, and eager to keep going. Now, imagine bringing that same energy and excitement into classrooms today. That’s what gamification in education is all about—turning learning into something that feels less like a chore and more like a fun challenge or adventure.

Education doesn’t have to be about long lectures, dry textbooks, and memorizing facts. And gamification doesn’t mean replacing these lessons with playtime. Instead, it’s like sprinkling a little bit of fun onto the work. Think of it as taking the best parts of games—earning rewards, leveling up, and even a little friendly competition—into the classroom to make learning feel more engaging and exciting.

This guide explores some of the best gamification strategies educators can use to spark engagement, boost motivation, and make learning something students actually look forward to.

What Is Gamification in Education?
----------------------------------

Gamification in education applies game-like elements to a curriculum to make learning more interactive. It takes the fun features we usually see in games—like earning points, collecting badges, tracking leaderboards, and completing challenges—and adds them to everyday teaching and learning activities.

Instead of simply completing assignments or studying for tests, students are rewarded for their efforts with things like virtual badges, progress bars, or even a chance to “level up.” These rewards provide additional, attainable goals that motivate students and make learning feel more like a fun challenge than a chore.

Gamification can help students focus better, work together with others, and stay excited about their progress. It also gives quick feedback so they can see how well they’re doing and what they need to work on.

Gamification vs. Game-Based Learning
------------------------------------

Gamification and game-based learning are both ways to make education more fun and exciting, but they are quite different. Gamification learning is about adding game-like elements to regular learning activities. The content of the lesson stays the same, but it’s made more interesting. For example, in a science class, students might earn badges for completing experiments or see their name on a leaderboard for scoring well on a quiz. The goal is to keep students motivated and encourage them to participate by turning everyday tasks into fun challenges.

Game-based learning, on the other hand, involves using real games to teach. These games—whether they’re digital, physical board games, or even role-playing activities—are designed to help students learn as they play. The game itself becomes the main tool for teaching. For example, a geography game might have students solve puzzles about different countries, or a history game could have them take on the role of historical characters to learn about major events. In game-based learning, students dive into the game, solve problems, and complete tasks that are directly connected to the lesson.

Both approaches are great tools for teachers, and they work in different ways. Gamification is about making daily lessons more exciting, which is great for encouraging effort and participation, tracking progress, and rewarding students for their work. Game-based learning, however, is more about immersing students in a topic. It helps them explore subjects deeply and learn by solving problems, thinking critically, and working together.

Key Benefits of Gamifying Educational Experiences
-------------------------------------------------

Research shows that adding game elements to learning can make it much more effective and enjoyable. Here are some key benefits of gamification in education:

*   **Increases student engagement**: Gamification turns learning into an interactive and fun experience by using elements like points, badges, and challenges. A [study by Hakulinen and Auvinen](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/6821820) found that students stay more focused and actively participate when learning feels like a game.
*   **Boosts motivation**: Rewards, leaderboards, and challenges encourage students to push themselves. [Researchers observed](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/07356331221127635) that recognizing progress and fostering friendly competition helps students stay motivated and strive to achieve more.
*   **Improves knowledge retention:**Interactive gamified environments help students retain information by making learning immersive and engaging. A [study by Carsten Busch](https://books.google.com.ph/books?hl=en&lr=&id=IedEBQAAQBAJ&oi=fnd&pg=PA50&ots=bHXf4U0oZ3&sig=G9RV6PSZE_5FX5jFZJExcw-xq8g&redir_esc=y#v=onepage&q&f=false) found that students who participated in gamified activities revisited topics more frequently and remembered material mo

enriched_points.expanded_content: Points are awarded for completing tasks, encouraging continuous engagement and providing immediate feedback, similar to traditional grading but more motivating and game-like.
Badges serve as digital tokens of accomplishment, acknowledging milestones and encouraging participation, while fostering a sense of pride and community among learners.
Leaderboards introduce a competitive element, showcasing learners' progress and encouraging healthy rivalry, which can enhance motivation and drive engagement through social comparison.
Quests turn learning objectives into engaging narratives, prompting students to embark on challenges that require problem-solving, fostering deeper engagement with the content.
Challenges provide opportunities for learners to apply their skills in practical situations, promoting critical thinking and enhancing learning through experiential, game-like scenarios.
